coredata.dc:title: Copper doped carbon dots modified bacterial cellulose with enhanced antibacterial and immune regulatory functions for accelerating wound healing

coredata.dc:description: The microenvironment of wound healing is susceptible to bacterial infection, chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and inadequate angiogenesis, requiring the development of innovative wound dressings with antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and angiogenic capabilities. This research crafted a new multifunctional bacterial cellulose composite membrane infused with copper-doped carbon dots (BC/Cu(II)-RCDs). Findings validated the successful loading of copper-doped carbon dots onto the BC membrane via hydrogen bonding interactions. Compared to the pure BC membrane, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane exhibited significantly enhanced hydrophilicity, tensile properties, and thermal stability. Diverse in vitro assays demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and antibacterial activity of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes, alongside their ability to expedite the inflammatory phase and stimulate angiogenesis. In vivo trials corroborated the membrane's ability to foster epithelial regeneration, collagen deposition, and tissue regrowth in full-thickness skin wounds in rats while also curbing inflammation in infected full-thickness skin wounds. More importantly, the treatment of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane may result in the activation of VEGF and MAPK signaling proteins, which are key players in cell migration, angiogenesis, and skin tissue development. In essence, the developed BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane shows promise for treating infected wounds and serves as a viable alternative material for medicinal bandages.

The cellulose layer produced by Trichoderma has regular tissues and large cavities; (d) SEM image of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane magnified 6000 times; (e) EDS spectrum of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 showing different colors; (f) FT-IR spectra of the BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane; (g) XRD pattern of the BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160; (h-i) TGA and DTG curves of the BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane; (j) The tensile stress–strain curves of BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane; (k)The compressive stress–strain curves of BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane; (l-m) BC/Cu(II)-RCD160 composite membrane subjected to a weight of 200 g and 500 g; (n) Water contact angle images of BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCD160 composite membrane.

(g) Images of Live/dead staining of E. coli and (h) S. aureus cultured on BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membranes for 24 h; (i–j) Relative viability of E. coli and S. aureus after exposure to various Cu(II)-RCDs concentrations; (k–l) Relative viability of E. coli and S. aureus after exposure to different concentrations of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membranes using the plate count method; (m–n) Relative viability of E. coli and S. aureus after exposure to different concentrations of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membranes based on live/dead bacteria staining; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

Analysis of cell biological processes in HUVECs affected by BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane. (a) Principal component analysis (PCA) based on bulk RNA-seq results of the PBS and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160; (b) Heat map of mRNAs involved in the treatment progression; (c) The volcano map of the transcriptomic profiles; (d) Bar diagram of partial biology progress GO term clusters of DEGs; (e)Bubble diagram of differential genes that enriched in KEGG pathway; (f) The heatmap analysis of differentially expressed genes involved in multiple pathways.

Wound closure analysis in skin full-thickness defect SD rat models. (a) Illustration of the animal experiment plan; (b) representative digital photographs of wounds on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14. (c) Schematic diagram of wounds treated with different nanoparticles for 14 days. (d) Wound healing rates for the control, BC, Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 groups on days 3, 7, 10, and 14. (d) Wound healing rates for Control, BC, Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 on days 3, 7, 10, and 14;* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

Histology analysis for wound healing in skin full-thickness defect SD rat models on days 7 and 14. (a) Images of HE staining and Masson trichrome staining of wounds on the 7th and 14th day after surgery; (b) immunohistochemical staining images of INOS, CD206, CD31, and VEGF; quantitative analysis of HE at 7 (c) and 14 days (d); (e) 14-day Masson quantitative analysis; quantitative analysis of INOS (f) and CD206 levels (g), as well as CD31 (h) and VEGF levels (i) represent four independent experiments. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.
Schematic diagram of the mechanism of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane promotes the regeneration of damaged skin tissue through antibacterial, anti-inflammation, and antioxidant and angiogenesis.

head.ce:title: Copper doped carbon dots modified bacterial cellulose with enhanced antibacterial and immune regulatory functions for accelerating wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The microenvironment of wound healing is susceptible to bacterial infection, chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and inadequate angiogenesis, requiring the development of innovative wound dressings with antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and angiogenic capabilities. This research crafted a new multifunctional bacterial cellulose composite membrane infused with copper-doped carbon dots (BC/Cu(II)-RCDs). Findings validated the successful loading of copper-doped carbon dots onto the BC membrane via hydrogen bonding interactions. Compared to the pure BC membrane, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane exhibited significantly enhanced hydrophilicity, tensile properties, and thermal stability. Diverse in vitro assays demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and antibacterial activity of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes, alongside their ability to expedite the inflammatory phase and stimulate angiogenesis. In vivo trials corroborated the membrane's ability to foster epithelial regeneration, collagen deposition, and tissue regrowth in full-thickness skin wounds in rats while also curbing inflammation in infected full-thickness skin wounds. More importantly, the treatment of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane may result in the activation of VEGF and MAPK signaling proteins, which are key players in cell migration, angiogenesis, and skin tissue development. In essence, the developed BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane shows promise for treating infected wounds and serves as a viable alternative material for medicinal bandages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin serves as the primary barrier against the external environment. Intact skin not only safeguards the body from external stimuli and microbial invasions but also prevents the loss of body fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, as well as damage to other organs. When the skin is extensively damaged or deeply damaged, the wound is difficult to heal on its own (Mayet et al., 2014). The dynamic process of wound repair involves four primary phases: hemostasis, inflammation, angiogenesis, and tissue remodeling (Yuan et al., 2023). Traditional dressings, such as gauze and cotton wool, are commonly used for wound treatment; however, their dry nature can lead to adherence to the wound surface, potentially causing additional injury upon removal (Khalid et al., 2017). In order to protect wounds from infections and external environmental stimuli and to speed healing from a variety of angles, a novel type of wet dressing that is antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-adhesion is desperately needed. Recently, a variety of materials have been developed for wound repair, including foam (Xie et al., 2022), nanofiber (Martin et al., 2022), hydrogel (Eivazzadeh-Keihan, Ahmadpour, et al., 2021), and functional gauze (Montaser et al., 2020). Among these, polysaccharide-based hydrogels are considered highly advantageous due to their extracellular matrix-like properties for cell adhesion, superior moisture retention for wound repair, and ease of removal to avoid secondary injury. Particularly, natural polymer-based hydrogels such as agar (Eivazzadeh-Keihan, 2021), cellulose (Eivazzadeh-Keihan, Khalili, et al., 2021), pectin (Eivazzadeh-Keihan, Ahmadpour, et al., 2021), alginate (Eivazzadeh-Keihan et al., 2020), and chitosan (Bano et al., 2017) have garnered significant interest from researchers due to their outstanding biocompatibility. Of these, cellulose emerges as the most prevalent natural polymer-based hydrogel. Bacterial cellulose (BC), a natural hydrogel produced and secreted by Acetobacter xylinum, offers exceptional mechanical properties, high crystallinity, high porosity, non-toxicity, and low immunogenicity. Most importantly, BC keeps the wound surface wet, sticks to it well, and absorbs exudates efficiently. These characteristics make BC an excellent material for tissue engineering and wound dressings because they encourage the development of epithelial cells and the creation of new tissue (Wahid et al., 2021). However, BC lacks inherent antimicrobial properties to prevent infections, necessitating its modification for ideal wound dressing applications. It is fortunate that BC's composition of nanofibers, with a specific width of <100 nm, provides a vast specific surface area and high porosity. This makes it an ideal matrix for drug delivery. To improve BC's efficacy as physiologically active BC-based antibacterial dressings, a variety of antibacterial agents have recently been added (He et al., 2021). These agents mainly include natural and synthetic polymers, such as chitosan (Li et al., 2024; Liao et al., 2023), alginate (Ji et al., 2021; Shahriari-Khalaji et al., 2020), collagen (Pasaribu et al., 2020), gelatin (Mao et al., 2021), silk sericin (Napavichayanun et al., 2018), polyethylene glycol (PEG) (Wang et al., 2019), cationic antimicrobial agents (Wei et al., 2011), antibiotics (Volova et al., 2018), and metal/metal oxide nanoparticles (NP) (such as Au, Cu, Ag, Zn, etc.) (Araújo et al., 2018; Candra et al., 2024; Dincă et al., 2020; Gupta et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2022).

Among all antibacterial metals incorporated into BC membranes, copper is notable for balancing antibacterial activity and biocompatibility (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022). It exhibits stronger antibacterial properties than metals such as zinc and gold and higher cell tolerance concentrations than silver and zinc (Kawakami et al., 2008). Copper is preferable for clinical applications over gold and silver due to its high natural abundance, accessibility, and low cost. It has recently been demonstrated that copper stimulates the production of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) by bone marrow-derived stem cells, promoting cell proliferation, colloidal deposition, and the inward development of new blood vessels (Li et al., 2023), thereby making copper an ideal metal for wound treatment. However, copper-based nanomaterials are difficult to degrade, may exhibit potential biological toxicity, and do not simultaneously resist oxidative stress (Qu et al., 2023). Therefore, it is crucial to develop a multifunctional copper-doped nanoantibacterial material with high biocompatibility, free radical scavenging capabilities, and superior antibacterial activity.

Studies have shown that doping Cu(II) with carbon dots (CDs) can improve the chemical defects that Cu(II) is difficult to degrade (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022; Miao et al., 2023; Najaflu et al., 2022). CDs are nanomaterials with a dimensionality of <10 nm. These materials are becoming more and more well-known in the biomedical industry because of their exceptional qualities, which include high quantum yield, outstanding biocompatibility, biodegradability, photoluminescence, minimal cytotoxicity, and high chemical stability (Omidi et al., 2017). Notably, the ultrasmall size of CDs provides more exposed active sites and functional groups, with surfaces containing epoxy, amino, ether, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups. These functional groups not only enhance the reactivity and hydrophilicity of CDs (Liu, Xu, et al., 2022) but also make CDs easy to further functionalize through atom doping, surface modification, and combination with other nanomaterials. Furthermore, CDs are easily synthesized from any organic substance containing elemental carbon. Hence, CDs are viewed as an optimal choice for pairing with Cu(II).

Currently, the preparation of carbon dots using polyphenol groups as raw materials has emerged as a prominent topic (Han, Hong, et al., 2023).Using polyphenols, naturally occurring plant compounds, as carbon sources supports a green and environmentally friendly synthesis process that adheres to the principles of sustainable materials research. Carbon dots derived from polyphenols exhibit improved water dispersibility, biocompatibility, antioxidant activity, and anti-inflammatory effects compared to polyphenols alone (Deng et al., 2023; Gudimella et al., 2022). Additionally, the conversion of polyphenols into carbon dots can enhance their bioavailability and pharmacological efficacy. Resveratrol (3,5,4′-trihydroxy silene), a naturally occurring nonflavonoid polyphenolic compound found in plants and a carbon-containing organic compound (Jia et al., 2022), is recognized for containing vital nutrients and bioactive substances such as minerals, vitamins, and antioxidants. It has exhibited anti-inflammatory and angiogenic regulating qualities, both of which are advantageous for hastening the healing of wounds (Ma et al., 2018).

For these reasons, resveratrol was selected as the natural polyphenol carbon source, and Cu(II) as the metal source, to synthesize copper-doped resveratrol carbon dots (Cu(II)-RCDs). Investigating whether Cu(II)-RCDs can enhance their anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and angiogenic pharmacological effects, as well as provide broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and improved biocompatibility, is warranted. Moreover, the small size and multiple functional groups of carbon dots allow Cu(II)-RCDs to effectively coordinate and bind to the cavity of BC, making them suitable as wound healing materials in BC. To date, composite BC membranes incorporating only resveratrol and Cu have been extensively reported (He et al., 2018; Meng et al., 2019; Shao et al., 2016); however, surface functionalization of BC using copper-doped carbon dots has not yet been documented.

The innovative aspect of this work is the synthesis of BC impregnated with Cu(II)-RCDs as a material for wound healing. Despite the multifunctionality of copper-containing nanoparticles, skin-compatible media are essential for wound dressings to safeguard the wound and foster a conducive healing environment. By stabilizing multifunctional nanoparticles and sustaining Cu(II) release, BC serves as a carrier for multifunctional copper nanomaterials, reducing the cytotoxicity of Cu(II).

In this study, copper-doped resveratrol carbon dots were first synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes were then prepared by impregnating the BC membrane with Cu(II)-RCDs. It is hypothesized that the surface of Cu(II)-RCDs, rich in carboxyl groups, binds to Cu(II) through hydrogen bonding interaction (Najaflu et al., 2022). Previous research suggests that Cu(II)-RCDs may adhere to the surface of BC via van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding interactions, or electrostatic interactions, which could slow the release rate of Cu(II), decrease toxicity, and enhance the hydrophilicity of BC (Kousheh et al., 2020; Zor et al., 2018). Moreover, owing to the synergistic antibacterial behavior of resveratrol and Cu(II), BC composite membranes with Cu(II)-RCDs deposition are expected to exhibit effective antibacterial activity. Using plate counting, disk diffusion, and live/dead bacterial staining techniques, the antibacterial properties of Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were assessed. In vitro cell experiments were conducted to assess the potential cytotoxicity and to explore the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and angiogenic properties of Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes on L929 cells, HUVECs, and RAW264.7 cells. Further research focused on the ability of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes to promote full-thickness wound healing in SD rats.

CuCl2·2H2O, NaOH, and resveratrol were obtained from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. BC was purchased from Hainan Yide Food Co., Ltd. (Hainan, China). A calcein AM/PI double staining kit was acquired from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC), mouse fibroblasts (L929), and mouse monocyte macrophage leukemia cells (RAW264.7) were sourced from the laboratory of West China School of Stomatology, Sichuan University (Chengdu, China). Antibiotics (targeting CD31, VEGF, iNOS, CDs206, TNF-α, and Arg-1) were obtained from Abcam (UK). Chemicals of analytical grade were employed in this work without any additional purification.

BC membranes were produced by Acetobacter xylinum and obtained from Hainan Yida Food Co. Ltd., China. The membranes were cultivated in a nutritional medium including sugar, coconut water, glacial acetic acid, and citric acid for a duration of seven days to produce BC (He et al., 2023). The BC membrane utilized in this investigation was a hydrogel with an approximate thickness of 3 mm. The purification process of bacterial cellulose followed the procedure proposed by He et al. (2018). In summary, distilled water was used three times to wash the bacterial cellulose in order to get rid of contaminants. The recovered BC was then cooked for 1 h in 1 % NaOH until it turned white, signifying the removal of additional impurities. The sample was then rinsed with deionized water until the wash solution's pH was neutral. Post-neutralization, the membranes were ready for further analysis, wherein absorbent filter paper was employed to extract water from the purified BC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of composite membranes in this study followed the method proposed by Khalid et al. (2017) and Kousheh et al. (2020). Initially, Cu(II)-RCDs aqueous solutions were prepared with concentrations of 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL using freeze-dried Cu(II)-RCDs powder and a PBS solution. Subsequently, the purified BC membrane was cut into circular slices with a 15 mm diameter, immersed in the Cu(II)-RCDs solutions, and agitated with a magnetic stirrer for 48 h. Following this, the samples underwent thorough rinsing with deionized water to eliminate any residual chemical substances from the surface and were then drained to prepare the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane. The membranes were soaked in Cu(II)-RCDs solutions of 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL. These membranes were named BC/Cu(II)-RCDs20, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs40, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs80, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320, respectively. Finally, they were subjected to freeze-drying for 48 h before further analysis and utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectroscopy (Varian Cary, USA) was used to detect the absorption peaks of the RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles, and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS55, USA) was used to detect their fluorescence properties, with a scanning range of 200–800 nm. The chemical elemental composition of the nanoparticles was studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, Germany) was used to detect the functional groups of the nanoparticles in the spectral range of 4000 to 500 cm−1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6510LV, JEOL, Japan) was used to observe the surface morphology of the nanoparticles at 15.0 kV. The elemental composition of the nanoparticles was analyzed through X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with SEM. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 TF0, Netherlands) was used to observe the surface morphology and lattice spacing of the samples. Physical analysis of the crystal structure of the nanoparticles was performed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker AXS GmbH, Germany). The zeta potential of the nanoparticles was measured using a Zetasizer (Nano ZSE, Malvern Instruments, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The functional group changes of the sample were evaluated using FTIR in the spectral range of 4000 to 600 cm−1. By observing the surface morphology and structure of the samples via SEM, the prepared BC and BC/Cu (II)-RCDs160 samples were freeze-dried, cut into 0.5 mm thick sections, and sputtered with a gold layer. Then, the samples were observed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, Japan). The elemental composition of the sample was analyzed using an X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) coupled with SEM. Physical analysis of the crystal structure of the sample was performed through XRD. The surface wettability of the freeze-dried BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane was measured by the sessile-drop method using a contact angle meter (OCA20, Dataphysics Inc., Germany). The composite membranes' thermal stability was assessed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Setaram Inc., France). To assess the sample's thermal stability, the freeze-dried material was heated under nitrogen environment from room temperature to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial evaluation of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: Plate counting was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the BC membrane, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane, and various concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs solutions (0, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) against E. coli (ATCC 25932) and S. aureus (ATCC 25923). Cu(II)-RCD solutions in varying concentrations (0, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) or a circular BC composite membrane (10 mm in diameter) were put into a 24-well plate and UV light-sanitized. The sample was mixed with a 2 mL (1 × 106 CFU/mL) bacterial suspension, which was kept at 37 °C for 24 h. Bacteria that had not been treated served as blank controls. Following that, Luria broth matrix (LB) medium was used to dilute the supernatant 10,000 times. Next, a standard nutritional agar plate was coated with 100 μL of the aliquot, and it was incubated for a whole day. Next, the colonies were photographed and counted. To calculate the antibacterial rate, utilize the following formula:
C and E are the bacterial counts of the control and experimental groups, respectively.

This research entails evenly distributing 100 μL of E. coli or S. aureus suspension (each at 1 × 107 CFU/mL) on a nutrient agar plate. Then, sterile circular filter paper, 6 mm in diameter, will be fully submerged in various Cu(II)-RCDs solution concentrations (40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) for 2 h. Following this, the filter paper sheets, soaked in different Cu(II)-RCDs concentrations, will be placed on a solid culture medium and incubated for 24 h. Additionally, UV-sterilized BC membranes and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs nanocomposite membranes will be cut into 6 mm circular plates and positioned on agar plates containing the specified bacterial strains. After that, these plates will be incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Lastly, the diameter of the inhibition zone will be determined and photographs will be taken in order to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane and Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles.

As detailed in Section 2.6.1, the samples (BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320, and solutions with varying concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs (0, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320 μg/mL) underwent processing. These materials were co-cultured for 6 h with a suspension of E. coli or S. aureus (108 CFU/mL). Subsequently, bacteria were collected via centrifugation and stained with calcein AM/PI double-staining reagents before settling for 30 min. Following this, a laser-scanning confocal microscope was utilized to observe the stained microorganisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization and biocompatibility of Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs
CCK8 assay of Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: We employed the CCK-8 assay to assess the cell biocompatibility of the Cu(II)-RCDs to determine their impact on cell proliferation. L929 cells, HUVECs, and RAW264.7 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates at densities of 1 × 104, 3 × 104, and 1 × 104 cells per well, respectively. After cell adhesion, the original culture medium was discarded, and different concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs (0, 20, 40, 80, 160, or 320 μg/mL) were added. The wells were rinsed with PBS following 1, 3, and 5 days of incubation in a cell culture incubator. Then, each well was filled with 100 μL of primary culture media. After that, a specific quantity of CCK-8 reagent was applied. Each well's absorbance was measured at 450 nm a few hours later.

Moreover, the cell biocompatibility of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes was investigated using the CCK-8 method. Prior to testing, both BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes underwent sterilization via ultraviolet light irradiation. Subsequently, sample extracts were prepared following the international standard ISO10993-12. This involved immersing the samples in Eagle's minimum essential culture medium (EMEM, ATCC30-2003) at 37 °C with a stirring speed of 120 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 24 h. The extraction ratio was maintained at 3 cm2 of material per 1 mL of EMEM solution to produce the leaching solution of the BC membrane, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membrane. 96-well plates were seeded with L929 cells, HUVECs, and RAW264.7 at densities of 1 × 104, 3 × 104, and 1 × 104 cells per well, in that order. The experimental group was given three distinct extracts after a 24-hour period of cell adhesion, whereas the control group was given equal volumes of whole media. The remaining experimental techniques were the same as those outlined for the CCK-8 experiment employing Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles, and each group experienced six repetitions.

The HUVECs and L929 cells were seeded at the same density as that utilized for the previously mentioned CCK-8 assay. The cells were cultivated for one, three, or five days in DMEM supplemented with varying doses of Cu(II)-RCDs solution or the extracting solution of BC composite membrane as the culture medium. The cells were stained with AM live-cell dye for 30 min at 37 °C in a cell culture incubator after the liquid component was removed. The cells were cleaned in PBS after removing the liquid component, and their viability was assessed using a fluorescent inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological functions of Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: L929 and HUVECs cells were inoculated at densities of 2 × 105 cells and 4 × 105 cells per well in each well of a 6-well plate to simulate a fused cell monolayer. After the cells had connected to the plate's bottom, a line was drawn with the tip of a pipette. Then, a low serum-conditioned medium was added, and scratches were created on the fused cell monolayer. After three PBS rinses, 2 mL of DMEM with solutions containing varying concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs (20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) or the extraction medium of the BC composite membrane were added. Following a 24-hour culture period, the cells were subjected to a 15-minute calcein-AM dye incubation period, followed by fluorescence microscopy observation. Using ImageJ software, a quantitative analysis was carried out to identify the initial scratch area (M0) and the unhealed scratch region (M1). The cell migration rate was determined with the following equation:
M0 and M1 represent the initial and unhealed scratch areas, respectively.

In a 24-well transwell plate, 2 × 104 L929 and 4 × 104 HUVEC cells were seeded in each well of the upper chamber to perform the transwell migration assay. The membrane was placed at the bottom of the plate. Next, 500 μL of full growth medium (including varying concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs solution (20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) or the extraction medium of the BC composite membrane was added to the lower chamber and 300 μL of culture medium was added to the upper chamber. The mobility of the cells was studied after 12 and 24 h.

The animal experiments adhered to the ARRIVE guidelines and were conducted in line with the ethical standards set by the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee of Shanxi Medical University (Permission No. KQDW-2024-001). Twenty male SD mice, aged 6–8 weeks and weighing 250 ± 20 g, were sourced from the Animal Experiment Center of Shanxi Medical University in Taiyuan, Shanxi (license no. SCXK (Jin) 2019–0007). They were housed under standard light-dark conditions with ad libitum access to water and a balanced diet as per a predetermined schedule. All procedures involving animals were performed in accordance with the U.K. Animals (Scientific Procedures) Act, 1986, and its associated guidelines.

To put it briefly, twenty male SD rats were given an intraperitoneal injection of ketamine (100 mg/kg body weight) to induce anesthesia and drowsiness. Subsequently, each rat's dorsal region had four surgically produced circular full-thickness incisions, each measuring roughly 1 cm in diameter. The surgical procedure strictly adhered to sterile principles to minimize the risk of infection. Subsequently, the rats were divided into four groups: the blank group, BC group, Cu(II)-RCDs solution group (Cu(II)-RCDs160), and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group. The materials were disinfected separately, and the wounds were placed on the backs of the rats in each group of SD rats. Wounds without covering materials were used as the control group. Then, all wounds were covered with gauze and secured with adhesive bandages. On the third, seventh, tenth, and fourteenth days following surgery, the materials were replaced, pictures of the wounds were taken, and ImageJ software was used to assess the wounds. After determining the affected areas, the following formula was used to compute the wound healing rates:
where R0 is the wound area on day 0, and Rx is the wound area on day x (x = 3, 7, 10, 14).

On days 3 and 7, some of the rats were euthanized, while the remaining ones were euthanized on day 14 to collect wound tissue samples. Subsequently, these samples underwent a series of treatments, including immersion in 4 % paraformaldehyde, sequential exposure to ethanol solutions of increasing concentrations (50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 %), and finally in xylene. The obtained wound tissue was then embedded in paraffin wax and sliced into 5 μm sections, which were further processed through degreasing and rehydration. Sections from days 7 and 14 underwent staining with hematoxylin-eosin (HE) and Masson's trichrome to evaluate epithelial regeneration and collagen deposition. Immunohistochemical staining for INOS, CD206, VEGF, and CD31 was performed on day 14 tissue samples to assess inflammation and vascular regeneration. Dihydroethidium (DHE) was utilized to indicate ROS levels at the wound site, with fluorescence excited at 525 nm and collected within the 600–625 nm range.

Statistical analysis was conducted using GraphPad Prism 9 software. The data are expressed as means ± standard deviations (n = 3–5). GraphPad Prism and Origin 2021 software programs were employed to generate the final figures. A significance criterion of p < 0.05 (*) was applied to all data analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: This study produced fluorescent copper-doped resveratrol carbon dots (Cu(II)-RCDs) via a one-step hydrothermal process, utilizing resveratrol as the carbon source and CuCl2·2H2O as the metal source (Fig. 1 ).

TEM revealed that the Cu(II)-RCDs were predominantly spherical, with uniform sizes and good dispersion (Fig. 2 (a–c)). Their diameters ranged from 1.1 to 3.52 nm, averaging 2.32 nm. The High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2(c)) indicated a lattice spacing for the Cu(II)-RCDs of approximately 0.18 nm, suggesting a narrow and uniform particle size distribution. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, displayed in Fig. 2(d), were utilized to analyze the surface functional groups of the RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs. The absorption peaks at 3410 cm−1 are linked to the stretching vibrations of the OH group in the carboxyl group (Sun et al., 2016), which aids the coordination between copper and –COOH (Najaflu et al., 2022). Additionally, the peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C = O bond in the carboxyl group (Yao et al., n.d.; Raveendran et al., 2019), while the peak at 1511 cm−1 is due to the vibrations of the C = C bond in the aromatic ring. Peaks at 1157 cm−1 and 833 cm−1 relate to CO stretching vibrations and CH out-of-plane bending vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the weak peak at 539 cm−1 is attributed to the Cu-O//Cu-N group (Mu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020), signifying, indicating the successful formation of the Cu coordination compound (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022). The doping of Cu did not affect the surface. The functional groups or the positions of the absorption peaks of the RES-CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2(d), indicate the preservation of the original functional groups of the RES-CDs. To further examine the composition and chemical ratio of the Cu(II)-RCDs, XPS and EDS mapping analyses were conducted. The elemental composition and chemical state of the Cu(II)-RCDs (Fig. 2(e–i)) and RES-CDs (Fig. S3(a–d)) were determined using XPS technology. The XPS full spectra in Figs. 2(e) and S3(a) show that the RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs mainly consist of C, O, N, and Cu. The atomic percentages of the RES-CDs are 63.33 %, 29.56 %, 6.91 %, and 0.2 %, while those of the Cu (II)-RCDs are 63.05 %, 31.4 %, 4.39 %, and 1.15 %, respectively. The increase in Cu content from 0.2 % to 1.15 % in the Cu(II)-RCDs, following the addition of CuCl2·H2O, indicates successful doping with Cu. The high-resolution spectra of each element in the Cu (II)-RCDs are shown in Fig. 2(f–i). The Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) features two prominent peaks, with the peak at 933.4 eV attributed to the Cu 2p3/2 characteristics of Cu+ and Cu2+ and the peak at 953.2 eV to the Cu 2p1/2 characteristics of Cu+ (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022). The high-resolution image of N1s (Fig. 2(g)) indicates that N predominantly exists in the forms of CN (401.9 eV) and NH (400 eV) (X. Yang et al., 2021). The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2(h)) shows the primary components of O as CO (532.9 eV) and OH (531.7 eV), while the high-resolution image of C1s (Fig. 2(i)) displays two main types of C: C = O/C-N (288.7 eV) and C-C/C = O (284.8 eV). EDS was employed to assess the surface roughness of the Cu(II)-RCDs (Fig. S2), revealing that N, C, O, and Cu constitute the majority of the surface composition, with C and O being more prevalent. In summary, the FTIR, XPS, and EDS/mapping results confirmed the successful doping of copper, and the FTIR results verified the presence of various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and nitrogen-containing groups, on the surface of the Cu(II)-RCDs. These outcomes underscore the potential applications of Cu(II)-RCDs, demonstrating their high hydrophilicity, water dispersibility, and ease of application.

Subsequent descriptions elucidate the fluorescence characteristics of the selected Cu(II)-RCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2(j), the Cu(II)-RCDs exhibit exceptional fluorescence performance. Under 365 nm UV light, they appear blue-green, while under natural light, they display a brownish-yellow color. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the Cu(II)-RCDs solution demonstrates an absorption peak at 233 nm, which is attributed to the π → π* transition of the C=C/C=O functional group on the surface of the Cu(II)-RCDs. Additionally, the absorption peak at 274 nm represents the(II) π-π* transition absorption typical of aromatic compounds. As depicted in Fig. 2(k), the Cu(II)-RCDs solution shows excitation-dependent PL characteristics. Under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the Cu(II)-RCDs produce strong yellow-green fluorescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 448 nm.

Fig. 2(l) depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of both RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs. Clear diffraction peaks are evident at 23.904° for RES-CDs and 22.90° for Cu(II)-RCDs, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022). These results imply satisfactory crystallinity of the graphitized CDs materials. In Fig. S1, the ζ potentials of the prepared RES-CDs and Cu(II)-RCDs are compared in water. The potentials measure −18.2 mV (negative ζ potential) and + 1.38 (positive ζ potential), respectively. Surface functional groups can create numerous emission traps, contributing to enhanced PL characteristics. Prior research indicates that positively charged CDs may offer superior benefits for antibacterial applications (Chai et al., 2021).
All these results demonstrate the successful synthesis of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization, mechanical, and adhesive performance of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 3(c), similar to the investigation conducted by Malmir et al. (2020), a highly porous structure is revealed by SEM for BC. High porosity and a well-organized, three-dimensional network structure with interconnected pores are characteristics of BC in its pure form. This pore dimension facilitates nanoparticle penetration into the cavities. Illustrated in Fig. 3(d–e), subsequent to functionalization with Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles, deposition of the nanoparticles onto or surrounding the nanofibers occurs. The EDS spectrum of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane is presented in Fig. 3(e), demonstrating an even distribution of C, O, and Cu across the membrane surface.

The FTIR results are shown in Fig. 3(f). For a pure BC membrane, 882 cm−1 is the absorption peak of the β-glycosidic bond of BC. Absorption peaks corresponding to OH and CH stretching vibrations are observed at 3345 cm−1 and 2895 cm−1, respectively. Previous studies suggest that Cu(II)-RCDs can be immobilized on BC membranes through the formation of hydrogen bonds (Salimi et al., 2021). Additionally, absorption peaks for CO, C-O-C, and -CH2 are identified at wavelengths of 1032 cm−1, 1161 cm−1, and 1427 cm−1, respectively (Castro et al., 2011; Mirtalebi et al., 2019). Moreover, compared with those of pure BC, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane has new absorption peaks at 1599 cm−1, 1512 cm−1, 1248 cm−1, and 1004 cm−1, where 1599 cm−1 corresponds to the amide I bond (C = O), 1512 cm−1 corresponds to the C = C bond, and 1248 cm−1 and 1004 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching vibration peak. In addition, compared with those of pure Cu(II)-RCDs (Fig. 2(d)), the elimination of OH tensile vibrations (3410 cm−1) in BC/Cu(II)-RCDs further confirmed that Cu(II)-RCDs and BC membranes bind through hydrogen bonding interactions (Kousheh et al., 2020).

The XRD patterns of the composite membranes made of pure BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs are displayed in Fig. 3(g). The wide crystalline peaks in the XRD pattern primarily indicate the semicrystalline characteristics of BC. The XRD pattern of the pure BC membrane shows three characteristic peaks located at 14.49°, 16.84° and 22.88° corresponding to the (110), (1-10), and (200) crystal planes of cellulose, namely, the I α and I β phases, which are similar to previous reports on BC membranes (Castro et al., 2011; He et al., 2018). In accordance with Bragg's law, the profile of BC exhibits a typical cellulose I crystal structure (Shezad et al., 2010). The XRD patterns of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs membranes also reveal distinct peaks characteristic of the cellulose I crystal, confirming that the crystal structure of BC remains unchanged after loading Cu(II)-RCDs. Preserving the exceptional performance of BC while creating composite membranes with Cu(II)-RCDs is crucial. Furthermore, two additional peaks at 2θ values of 50.30° and 73.61° are observed for the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes, corresponding to the (200) and (220) planes of the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure of Cu, respectively (Shao et al., 2016). These findings validate the successful deposition of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles on the three-dimensional BC nanofiber network. It is evident that all characteristic peaks of BC and Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles are accurately positioned in the composite material, with BC's crystallinity remaining largely unchanged after introducing Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles. Finally, it is noted that there is no clear evidence of CuO or Cu2O in the XRD patterns of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane. This absence may be attributed to the relatively low contents of Cu2O and CuO, resulting in their peak values being obscured in the 2θ = 30–40° range. Consequently, no corresponding XRD peaks are observed, indicating that Cu predominantly exists in nanoparticle form (Shao et al., 2016).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is a dependable method for studying the temperature-dependent thermal behavior, weight loss, and thermal degradation of materials (Muşat et al., 2014). As depicted in the TGA curve (Fig. 3(h)), both the BC membrane and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane exhibit a minor decrease in quality at 100 °C, primarily due to water evaporation from the sample (Eivazzadeh-Keihan, 2021). The TGA curve of BC reveals an initial degradation temperature of BC membrane is around 170 °C for the BC membrane. Consistent with Wahid et al.'s findings, the curve sharply drops, with the weight loss rate reaching approximately 60 % within the temperature range of 170 °C to 375 °C, mainly due to BC's thermal breakdown. This process involves further dehydration, depolymerization, disintegration of glucopyranosyl units, and subsequent oxidation, resulting in charred residue (Wahid et al., 2019). Although the weight loss curve of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane resembles that of the pure BC membrane, its initial degradation temperature is about 247 °C. Within the temperature range of 247–435 °C, the weight loss curve declines rapidly, with the weight loss rate reaching approximately 65 %. Beyond 435 °C, the gradual weight loss may be attributed to the loss of Cu(II)-RCDs. In the DTG curve, each temperature peak corresponds to the temperature at which the mass degradation rate of each material is highest during the weight loss process. As shown in Fig. 3(i), the maximal weight loss temperatures of the BC and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes are 319 °C and 353 °C, respectively. This suggests that at elevated temperatures, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane exhibits better thermal stability than the pure BC membrane. This enhancement may arise from the strong interaction between the Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles and BC, improving compatibility between the two components in the blend system. Consequently, overcoming greater steric barriers necessitates the fracture and degradation of macromolecules, thereby enhancing thermal stability.

To further explore the hydrophilicity characteristics of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane as a wound dressing, we conducted a comparison of contact angle results between the pure BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane. According to the contact angle experiment (Fig. 3(n)), the water contact angles of water drops on the dry pure BC hydrogel and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 after 20 s were 18.8° and 7.1°, respectively, suggesting an increase in the hydrophilicity of BC after the introduction of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles. This result can be attributed to the porosity structure of the BC membrane and the abundance of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups in the Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles.

Wound dressings should have appropriate mechanical strength to resist damage caused by external stress, making them stable when applied at the wound site (Yi, He, et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 3(l–m), the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane can withstand the weight of 200 g and 500 g while maintaining its flexibility. In addition, in order to further measure the mechanical strength of the composite membrane, tensile and compression tests were carried out. Fig. 3(j) shows the typical tensile stress-strain curves of pure BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160. The results show that compared with pure BC membrane, the tensile stress of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane is increased, and the tensile strain is decreased. Fig. 3(k) shows the typical compressive stress-strain curves of pure BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane. Both pure BC membrane and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane have good compressive stress-strain properties, and their compressive strains can reach >90 %. In addition, when the compression strain is 90 %, the compressive stress of pure BC membrane is 360 kPa, while that of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane can reach 420 kPa, which is slightly higher than that of pure BC membrane. This indicates that the addition of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles strengthens the hydrogen bond network in BC hydrogels and improves the final tensile strength, which is consistent with the literature (Tian et al., 2014; Malmir et al., 2020). These findings indicate the suitability of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes for wound dressings due to their hydrophilicity and mechanical properties (He et al., 2023).

As biomaterials, excellent adhesive performance is essential. BC itself demonstrates excellent adhesion properties at room temperature, firmly adhering to the volunteers' arms and wrists, and can be easily removed from skin tissue without causing irritation (Fig. S4(c)). In this study, the adhesion ability of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes to porcine skin was evaluated using a lap-shear test (Fig. S4(a)). As depicted in Fig. S4(b), BC hydrogel exhibits minimal adhesion strength on porcine skin. Conversely, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes exhibit higher adhesion strength of 7.28 ± 1.25 kPa due to the addition of carbon nanoparticles, surpassing the standard adhesion strength of commercial dressings (approximately 5 kPa) (H. Li et al., 2021). In addition, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane can be firmly glued to finger joints (bending angle from 0 to 180°) for a long time at different angles without any irritation and did not fall off during finger joints bending (Fig. S4(d)), it is shown that the composite membrane not only has good adhesion but also has good ductility, which is suitable for use as wound dressing (Yi, Cheng, et al., 2023). In terms of adhesion mechanism, the introduction of nanoparticles may enhance the hydrogen bond interaction of BC hydrogel network and promote the adhesion properties of composite membranes (Zhou et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial assay of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria serve as primary initiators of persistent wound infection and inflammation (He et al., 2022; Mou et al., 2022). Consequently, wound dressings must possess not only adequate mechanical strength and hydrophilicity but also antibacterial properties to thwart external bacterial invasion. Among various pathogens, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) are prominent bacterial strains responsible for wound infections. Consequently, they are extensively employed in studying the in vitro antibacterial efficacy of wound dressings.

Fig. 4 (a–b) present the evaluation of the antibacterial activity of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles against S. aureus and E. coli using the plate counting method, comparing them with the control group. Photographs depicting the colonies recultured with varying concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs are displayed in Fig. 4(a), while Fig. 4(i–j) show the antibacterial rates and bacterial counts of the different groups. In comparison with the blank control, low concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited a slight inhibitory effect on bacterial numbers, with their antibacterial activity increasing as the concentration rose. The antibacterial rates against E. coli for various concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs (20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μg/mL) were 80.5 % ±1.22 %, 97.62 % ±1.57 %, 99.45 % ±0.51 %, 99.75 % ±0.11 %, and 100.00 % ±0.00 %, respectively. Corresponding values for S. aureus were 56.71 % ±2.98 %, 91.1 % ±1.58 %, 99.61 % ±0.24 %, 100.00 % ±0.00 %, and 100.00 % ±0.00 %, respectively. These results indicate that Cu(II)-RCDs demonstrate a dose-dependent bactericidal effect (Fig. 4(a)). Compared with those of the pure BC membrane, the antibacterial activities of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 against E. coli and S. aureus were also evaluated using the plate counting method. Photographs of the colonies that recultured following co-culture with BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 are displayed in Fig. 4(b), and the antibacterial rates and bacterial counts of the various groups are displayed in Fig. 4(k–l). Compared with BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 had specific inhibitory effects on the number of bacteria. The antibacterial rates of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 against E. coli were 73.44 ± 1.48 % and 98.32 % ±1.08 %, respectively. The percentages for S. aureus were 96.03 % ±1.28 % and 99.94 % ±0.02 %, respectively.

Moreover, we examined the antibacterial efficacy of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles, BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 using the disk diffusion method. In this analysis, filter paper soaked in 40 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solutions did not exhibit any inhibitory regions (Fig. 4(c, e)), and similarly, the pure BC membrane did not demonstrate such regions (Fig. 4(d, f)), indicating limited antibacterial activity against S. aureus or E. coli. The diameter of the inhibitory zone progressively grew as Cu(II)-RCD concentrations rose. Notably, filter papers soaked in 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solution displayed inhibition zone diameters of 1.015 cm and 1.621 cm for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The filter papers soaked in the 320 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solution had inhibition zone diameters of 2.066 cm and 2.275 cm for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively (Fig. 4(c, e)). BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 also inhibited these two strains, with inhibition zone diameters of 2.166 cm and 1.725 cm for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, and 2.569 cm and 2.235 cm for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively (Fig. 4(d, f)).

Two different bacterial species that were cultivated on separate samples are shown in Fig. 4(g–h) with live/dead staining images; the green staining indicates live bacteria and the red staining indicates dead bacteria. As observed in the images, there is minimal red fluorescence signal detected in the pure BC group. However, with increasing Cu(II)-RCDs loading in the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes, the red fluorescence signal gradually intensifies. These observations indicate that the pure BC membrane lacks antibacterial activity against the tested strains, while the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane exhibits notable antibacterial activity against both S. aureus and E. coli. This suggests that Cu(II) released from the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane into the surrounding environment contributes to the potent bactericidal activity.

As an antibacterial dressing, the durability and stability of the antibacterial activity of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes are crucial in practical applications, and long-term release stability is required for these materials to act as antibacterial agents. The BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane exhibited remarkable antibacterial activity against both gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria, as indicated by the antibacterial experiment described previously. Similar to the BC membrane loaded with CuNPs and ZnO NPs, this effect is attributable to the prolonged and stable release of Cu(II) from Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles inserted into the BC membrane (He et al., 2018; Shahmohammadi Jebel & Almasi, 2016). Because of its distinct fibrillar nanostructure and three-dimensional porous network structure, BC has been seen to have a considerable amount of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups and hydrogen bonds entangled inside and between its molecules. According to Almaida et al., these characteristics not only support the attachment of metal nanoparticles onto cellulose but also promote the gradual release of Cu(II)-RCDs, resulting in persistent and effective antibacterial performance (Almeida et al., 2014). In the current study, Cu(II)-RCDs were synthesized by doping Cu(II) with carbon quantum dots derived from resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound known for its antioxidant properties. This approach not only mitigated the ease of Cu(II) oxidation in air but also diminished its toxicity. Cu(II)-RCDs are either encapsulated within the layered structure of the BC membrane or absorbed onto the nanofibers of BC, ensuring the stability of Cu(II) release within the membrane (Fig. 2). Previous research has shown that the integration of Cu nanoparticles or CuO nanoparticles into BC serves as an effective antibacterial agent against a variety of bacteria, including S. aureus, E. coli, Bacillus subtilis, Stenotrophomonas maltophilus, Enterobacter cloacae, Streptococcus agalactiae, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (W. He et al., 2023; Shao et al., 2016). It has been established that Cu nanoparticles or CuO nanoparticles employ multiple antibacterial mechanisms. These mechanisms involve attaching to microbial DNA, blocking bacterial replication, and upsetting the thiol groups of metabolic enzymes in the bacterial electron transport chain, thereby causing bacterial deactivation in vitro (Zhang et al., 2010). Furthermore, copper ions interact with bacterial cell walls, enhancing cell membrane permeability, which consequently disrupts the cell membrane or solidifies protein structures or modifies enzyme functions, ultimately inducing cell death (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022). The release rate and sustained stability of Cu(II) in BC are influenced by various factors such as the degree and pore size of the three-dimensional network, the abundance of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups, the cross-linking of hydrogen bonds, the porosity, the distribution of pore sizes, and the specific surface area (Sannino et al., 2009). Additionally, the release rate of Cu(II) is contingent on the diffusion coefficient of ions through the gel network, governed by Fickian diffusion and substrate swelling (Adepu & Khandelwal, 2020; Hoare & Kohane, 2008).

Considering the potential applications of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane for in vivo wound healing, further cellular experiments are required to ascertain the optimal concentration of Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles for the composite membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization and biocompatibility of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes

ce:section.ce:para: As a potential nanomaterial for promoting wound healing, the biocompatibility of Cu(II)-RCDs is crucial. Using the CCK-8 assay, we assessed the biocompatibility of Cu(II)-RCDs in several cell types. The cellular activity of the Cu(II)-RCDs was tested using HUVECs, L929, and RAW264.7 cells, as shown in Fig. 5 (a–c). According to the CCK-8 data from days 1, 3, and 5, there was no discernible toxicity when the concentration of Cu(II)-RCDs rose from 0 to 320 μg/mL in comparison to the control group. Greater cell proliferation rate was observed in the concentration groups compared to the control group for all three cell types. Additionally, according to live/dead staining of HUVECs and L929 cells (Fig. 5(e–f)), Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited no significant cytotoxicity, indicating suitability for long-term cell growth. On the third day, significant proliferation of the three cell types was observed in the 160 μg/mL concentration group compared to other groups, particularly the L929 cell group. This proliferation enhancement may be attributed to the addition of resveratrol to Cu(II)-RCDs. However, proliferation dropped at 320 μg/mL, presumably due to certain cells not adjusting to the high material concentration. These results clearly demonstrate the high biosafety levels of the prepared Cu(II)-RCDs. Although other concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs did not induce cytotoxicity, 160 μg/mL was the most effective in promoting cell proliferation. Therefore, a concentration of 160 μg/mL of Cu(II)-RCDs solution combined with a BC membrane was selected to prepare BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 5(d) shows the survival ability of HUVECs, L929s, and RAW264.7 cells treated with pure BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membrane extracts for 24 h. Although there was a minor decrease in cell viability relative to that of the control group for pure BC, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320, the total cell survival rate was >85 %, suggesting that these composite membranes had acceptable biocompatibility. Furthermore, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane had higher cell survival than the other two membranes, which might be attributed to the release of Cu(II)-RCDs from the composite membrane, boosting cell proliferation. The results showed that by controlling the concentration of Cu(II)-RCDs, the BC composite membrane can be noncytotoxic, and its antibacterial performance (Ma et al., 2016), cell proliferation ability, and angiogenesis ability can be improved (Liu, Huang, et al., 2022).

The compatibility between Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes doped at different concentrations and blood cells was verified through hemolysis tests. According to Fig. 5(g–h), compared with the control group (saline solution), there was no significant blood cell rupture in the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes doped with different concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs. This suggests that red blood cells are not significantly harmed by either Cu(II)-RCDs or these BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes. In the supernatant absorbance test, the hemolysis rate of the experimental group was incredibly low—all were <5 %. These findings show that BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes and Cu(II)-RCDs have good blood biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL imaging of Cu(II)-RCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the distribution of Cu(II)-RCDs in HUVECs, L929s, and RAW264.7 cells, particularly in the nucleus and cytoskeleton, staining was conducted using Cu(II)-RCDs, DAPI, and ghost pen cyclic peptides emitting green, blue, and red fluorescence. As illustrated in Fig. 5(i–k), after 48 h of Cu(II)-RCDs addition, normal cell morphology was observed for all three cell types, indicating the absence of cytotoxicity. Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited bright green fluorescence at the corresponding excitation wavelength and were evenly dispersed throughout the cytoplasm, with minimal infiltration observed in the nucleus's core area. These findings suggest rapid cellular uptake of Cu(II)-RCDs with minimal impact on the nucleus. Moreover, the overlapping light and dark field images reveal that the fluorescence emitted by 160 μg/mL and 320 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs nearly fills the cytoplasmic region, with consistent fluorescence intensity. However, compared to 320 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited higher fluorescence intensity, indicating better penetration into the nucleus. These results indicate that Cu(II)-RCDs can effectively fluorescently label HUVECs, L929s, and RAW264.7 cells, with the strongest labeling observed in the 160 μg/mL group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory capacities of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections can produce reactive oxygen species, which can cause severe inflammation around the wound (Bal-Öztürk et al., 2021). This increased inflammation substantially heightens the likelihood of infection-related complications and diminishes the efficacy of wound healing. Most research has focused on employing fungicides, overlooking the surplus of oxidative stress in localized wounds, which can trigger severe inflammation and even cell necrosis (Bryan et al., 2012). Hence, to enhance the environment and expedite the mending of skin defects, surplus ROS must be eradicated.

The purpose of this investigation was to determine whether Cu(II)-RCDs could decrease intracellular ROS levels in situations of high oxidative stress using ROS detection probes (DCFH-DA). DCFH-DA can enter cells freely and convert into DCFH, which transforms into DCF upon ROS induction, indicating intracellular ROS levels (Z. He et al., 2022). RAW264.7 cells and HUVECs were pretreated with Cu(II)-RCDs, and a specific concentration of Lps solution was added to mimic an oxidative microenvironment, visualized using CLSM. As shown in Fig. S5(a–b), the Lps group emitted strong green fluorescence after 1-h Lps stimulation, while the control group showed no fluorescence, suggesting increased ROS. Notably, intracellular fluorescence intensity decreased further with higher Cu(II)-RCDs concentrations, indicating their superior ROS scavenging ability, possibly enhanced by the presence of resveratrol, a potent antioxidant with multiple -OH groups (Jia et al., 2022). Hence, Cu((II)-RCDs could absorb excess ROS in wounds, alleviating oxidative stress and enhancing wound healing. Additionally, we examined the in vitro antioxidant properties of BC composite membranes. Fig. 6 (a–b) reveals a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite film compared to the Lps group, indicating enhanced antioxidant capacity with Cu(II)-RCDs incorporation, as evident in Fig. S5.

M1 macrophages emit inflammatory substances such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β during the healing process of wounds. These substances have the potential to exacerbate the healing process by inducing inflammation, impeding the recruitment of fibroblasts and angiogenic cells, and so on. On the other hand, M2 macrophages secrete Arg-1 and IL-10, which can alleviate inflammation and expedite wound healing (Orecchioni et al., 2019). Therefore, the effects of Cu(II)-RCDs solution and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane on macrophage phenotype and subsequent inflammatory conditions were examined. Initially, the anti-inflammatory properties of Cu(II)-RCDs were assessed using the Arg-1 immunofluorescence labeling method. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence intensity of the 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs group exceeded that of the other groups, indicating a significant enhancement in the expression of the anti-inflammatory factor Arg-1.

Next, the ability of the Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu (II)-RCDs160 composite membranes to regulate inflammatory processes and macrophage phenotypes was evaluated through qRT–PCR and ELISA. Lipopolysaccharides (Lps, 1 μg/mL) are effective inflammatory response activators that can stimulate macrophage polarization toward the proinflammatory M1 phenotype (Yu et al., 2023). To evaluate the anti-inflammatory ability of 160 μg/mL Cu (II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, RAW 264.7 macrophages were first treated with Lps (1 μg/mL) for 12 h and then co-cultured with 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solution and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 extract for 24 h, after which their mRNA expression levels were evaluated by qRT–PCR. The mRNA expression between the BC group and the Lps-treated group showed minimal difference, suggesting that BC itself lacks anti-inflammatory activity. However, there was a significant upregulation in mRNA expression levels of the anti-inflammatory factor IL-10 in both the Cu(II)-RCDs160 group and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group compared to the BC group (Fig. 6(i)). Moreover, mRNA expression levels of the proinflammatory factors TNF-α (Fig. 6(g)) and IL-6 (Fig. 6(h)) were notably decreased, confirming the anti-inflammatory ability of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane in suppressing macrophage polarization due to the presence of Cu(II)-RCDs.

Furthermore, to delve deeper into the anti-inflammatory properties of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, we co-cultured RAW 264.7 macrophages treated with Lps (1 μg/mL) with extracts of 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes for 48 h to gather the supernatant. Subsequently, we employed a commercial ELISA kit for testing, revealing that the expression levels of the proinflammatory factors TNF-α (Fig. 6(j)) and IL-1β (Fig. 6(k)) were diminished in both the Cu(II)-RCDs160 group and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group, while the expression levels of the anti-inflammatory factor IL-10 were significantly elevated (Fig. 6(l)).

In summary, the experimental findings suggest that during the inflammatory phase of wound healing, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes possess the capability to promptly eliminate excess ROS in the wound microenvironment excess, while efficiently fostering macrophage polarization toward the pro-regenerative phenotype (M2). Consequently, this results in a reduction in the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) and an augmentation in the secretion of anti-inflammatory factors (IL-10 and Arg–1), thereby abbreviating the duration of the inflammatory response and expediting wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vitro migration and angiogenic capacities of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes
Migration and angiogenic capacities of the Cu(II)-RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The establishment of a new circulatory system is a crucial aspect of wound healing as it facilitates improved nutrient delivery and oxygen supply, marking a pivotal stage in the proliferative phase of wound recovery. Angiogenesis in vivo primarily includes endothelial cell proliferation and migration, along with the release of angiogenesis-related factors, constituting a multifaceted process involving numerous molecules (Cheng & Fu, 2018). Consequently, the endothelial cell scratch test, Transwell migration assay, vascular growth factor expression analysis, and tube formation assay have emerged as three conventional experimental approaches for evaluating angiogenesis (Šalandová et al., 2021).

To explore the migration of HUVECs (Fig. 7(a)) and L929 cells (Fig. 7(b)) treated with Cu(II)-RCDs, we initially conducted a cell scratch assay. Among all the groups, the 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs treatment group had the most stable cell migration performance, with a notable increase in cell migration rate. Additionally, the migration capability of HUVECs (Fig. 7(c)) and L929 cells (Fig. 7(d)) was further assessed using Transwell experiments. The results showed that the permeable cells at the bottom of the Transwell insert were more permeable than those in the other groups in the 160 μg/mL treatment group. The results of both studies showed that treatment groups treated with 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited increased cell migration.

CD31 and VEGF are recognized as key angiogenic factors. To assess the angiogenic potential of Cu(II)-RCDs, we utilized an immunofluorescence assay to analyze the expression of CD31 (Fig. 7(e)) and VEGF (Fig. 7(f)) across varying concentrations of Cu(II)-RCDs. The results indicated a substantial increase in CD31 and VEGF protein expression in HUVECs following treatment with 160 μg/mL. Moreover, the HUVEC cytoskeleton staining data revealed well-stretched cells in each group, suggesting satisfactory biocompatibility of Cu(II)-RCDs. Subsequently, we conducted statistical analyses, including scratch tests, migration assays, and immunofluorescence assays, on both HUVECs and L929 cells. As depicted in Fig. 7(g–h), the group treated with 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited superior scratch healing compared to other concentration groups in both cell types. Furthermore, Fig. 7(i–j) demonstrates that the 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solution significantly enhanced HUVEC and L929 cell migration at 12 and 24 h. Additionally, according to Fig. 7(k–l), 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs exhibited the most pronounced effect in promoting the expression of vascular factors CD31 and VEGF.

Cu(II)-RCDs nanoparticles have displayed favorable biocompatibility, antioxidant properties, anti-inflammatory effects, and the capability to stimulate cell migration and proliferation in various in vitro cell assays. Based on the results obtained, it is evident that the 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs group exhibited the most promising outcomes among all experimental groups. Thus, we opted to employ 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs combined with BC to fabricate a BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane, intending to validate its potential in enhancing the healing of soft tissue defects in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The migration and angiogenic capacities of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Successful wound recovery relies on the presence of ample newly formed blood vessels in granulation tissue, ensuring sufficient nutrition delivery. Thus, the satisfactory biocompatibility and angiogenic potential of dressings are essential factors in wound therapy (Han, Chen, et al., 2023). To further verify the contribution of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane to angiogenesis, we used a CCK-8 assay, a scratch test, a Transwell migration test, an immunofluorescence (VEGF) assay, and a tube formation experiment to evaluate the effects of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane on angiogenesis. First, we co-cultured HUVECs, L929s, and RAW264.7 cells with a pure BC membrane, 160 μg/mL Cu(II)-RCDs solution, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane mixed at the same concentration for 24 h. Later, we investigated the cell compatibility of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the viability of HUVECs, L929 cells, and RAW264.7 cells remained above 85 % after co-culture with BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160, indicating good cell compatibility. In addition, compared to the BC group, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group also exhibited increased proliferation of HUVECs and L929 cells (Fig. 8(a)).

Furthermore, we conducted scratch and Transwell experiments to assess the migration behavior of HUVECs post-treatment with BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 (Fig. 8(c–d)). Following a 24-h period, the cell migration rates of both the Cu(II) - RCDs group and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs group and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane group exceeded those of the control and BC groups, indicating the significant potential of Cu(II)-RCDs in actively enhancing cell proliferation and expediting wound healing. Resveratrol, a non-flavonoid polyphenolic compound, has demonstrated efficacy in promoting tissue regeneration (Jia et al., 2022), while BC exhibits outstanding cell adhesion properties and can sustain cell viability even under conditions of restricted migration, facilitating cellular transfer. Hence, the specific impact of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane on the proliferation and migration of HUVECs and L929 cells is primarily attributed to the synergistic interplay between resveratrol and BC.

Sufficient neovascularization is crucial for promoting wound healing. Tube formation experiments showed the potent angiogenic properties of these materials. As depicted in Fig. 8(e), following a 6-h incubation on a Matrigel substrate, HUVECs treated with Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 extracts exhibited robust connectivity, regulation, and formation of a network with luminal tubules, particularly notable in the Cu(II)-RCDs group. In contrast, the BC and control groups showed less tubular formations; most produced structures had dispersed punctate nodules or dendritic protrusions, and HUVECs seemed unconnected. This underscores that the levels of vascularization in the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs groups surpass those in the BC and control groups. Furthermore, to corroborate this finding, we conducted immunofluorescence staining on L929 cells (VEGF). Similarly, the average fluorescence intensity of the Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 treatment groups exceeded that of all control and BC groups, showing that the presence of Cu(II)-RCDs efficiently activated the release of VEGF, hence accelerating tube formation (Fig. 8(f)).

In summary, the aforementioned findings collectively demonstrate the superior capabilities of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 in promoting HUVECs and L929s migration, as well as tube formation. Moreover, the release of Cu(II)-RCDs proves advantageous for the angiogenesis phase, the third stage of skin repair.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptome sequencing analysis of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 treated HUVECs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the changes of HUVECs after BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 treatment, we performed transcriptomic analysis of HUVECs treated with PBS and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 respectively. Principal component analysis (PCA) revealed a consistent gene expression profile for the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 treatment group, which is clustered differently from the PBS control group, as illustrated in Fig. 9(a). Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were selected according to the threshold value of |log2 FC| ≥ 0 and the q-value ≤0.05.Treatment by BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 induced marked transcriptomic changes in HUVECs (Fig. 9(b)). The volcano plot in Fig. 9(c) showed 1428 up-regulated genes and 296 down-regulated genes between PBS and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group. All these revealed that a remarkable range of gene expression differences among the groups. We carried out gene ontological (GO) enrichment analysis, encompassing biological processes (BP), molecular functions (MF), and cell components (CC) classes, in order to further refine and analyze these DEGs. The top ten unique up-regulated or down-regulated enrichment elements in each class were displayed in Fig. 9(d). It mainly focused on epidermal tissue development, regulation of epithelial cell differentiation, positive regulation of angiogenesis, fibronectin binding, keratin filament, which closely correlated with the whole process of wound healing (Gong et al., 2024).

The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analysis was then applied to analyze potential signaling pathways. Fig. 9(e) shows the top 15 different up-regulated or down-regulated KEGG pathways and their associated enrichment statistics. Further, the expression changes of specific genes were shown in a complex heatmap (Fig. 9(f)).Pathway enrichment analysis by KEGG showed that DEGs significantly enriched in metabolism, focal adhesion, and biosynthesis of amino acids-related pathways, particularly in MAPK signaling pathway and VEGF signaling pathways were also enriched (Fig. 9(e)). It is well known that the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway plays a key regulatory role in promoting cell migration during wound healing (Wahedi et al., 2017, 2020). Similarly, The VEGF signaling pathway is critical for several aspects of wound healing, such as collagen formation and angiogenesis (Chen et al., 2019). Transcriptomic analysis revealed these pathway-related genes such as PDGFA, RPS6KA6, DUSP16 (MAPK signaling pathway) and VEGFA, PRKCG, JMJD7-PLA2G4B, SHC2, PTGS2, SH2D2A(VEGF signaling) pathway) were significantly up-regulated (Fig. 9(f)), these results suggest that BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 may promote cell angiogenesis by activating MAPK and VEGF signaling, thus accelerating the wound healing process, which further confirms the results of this study in vitro. In addition, in the inflammatory stage of wound healing, a large accumulation of reactive oxygen species at the wound site can lead to severe inflammatory response, promote the secretion of inflammatory factors and the activation of related pathways, and inhibit wound repair (Gong et al., 2024). In this study, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane was proved to be effective in relieving local wound oxidative stress, transcriptomic analysis also showed that genes related to inflammatory pathways, such as pro-inflammatory factors IL-6, CCL2 and PGAM5, were significantly down-regulated, anti-inflammatory factors such as IL-1β, TGFBR1, CDKN2B and BMPR1B were significantly up-regulated, which leads to reduced inflammation and tissue destruction. In addition, keratin filament-related genes (KRT5, KRTAP2–4) and fibronectin binding related genes (ITGB3, CTSK), which are critical for skin development, were significantly up-regulated.

In summary, BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane may jointly promote wound healing process through various pathways such as keratin filament formation, epithelial cell differentiation, fibronectin binding, MAPK, and VEGF signaling pathways. Next, we will further explore the effectiveness of this pathway through animal experiments.

Wound healing is expected to be accelerated by the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane's antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and angiogenic properties as well as its capacity to encourage HUVEC and L929 proliferation and good cellular biocompatibility. The therapeutic mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 12.

The impact of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes as wound dressings on infected wounds in SD rats was evaluated. Following in vitro antibacterial assessments (Fig. 4), the antibacterial efficacy of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes closely mirrored that of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 composite membranes. Additionally, hemolysis studies indicated both BC/Cu(II)-RCD160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCD320 are non-toxic. However, findings from the CCK-8 experiment demonstrated superior cell biocompatibility of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes compared to BC/Cu(II)-RCDs320 (Fig. 5(d)). Hence, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane was chosen for subsequent in vivo investigations.

The SD rat skin full-thickness defect model serves as a widely recognized animal model frequently employed to assess the therapeutic efficacy of materials (Xu et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2018). Following the development of full-layer skin wounds on their backs, rats were divided into four groups: the Control group, BC group, Cu(II)-RCDs160 group, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane group. Macroscopic images of the wounds were captured at various intervals (Fig. 10(b)). Over time, the size of the wounds decreased for all groups from day 3 to day 7. Notably, wounds, w treated with Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes exhibited more rapid closure compared to those treated with the control or BC alone, with the healing properties of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite materials being notably superior. The quantified wound closure rates for the four groups are depicted in Fig. 10(c, d). All wounds demonstrated accelerated healing rates during the first 10 days post-operation, with the wound closure rates on day 10 for the control group, BC group, Cu(II)-RCDs160 group, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group being 54.57 ± 1.13 %, 60.66 ± 11.90 %, 70.56 ± 1.22 %, and 81.48 ± 1.13 %, respectively. It is noteworthy that wounds in the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane group were nearly completely healed by day 14, exhibiting a wound closure rate of 92.3 ± 0.41 %.
