In contrast, the control, BC, and Cu(II)-RCDs160 solution groups did not achieve full wound closure, with wound closure rates of 66.46 ± 1.57 %, 71.78 ± 0.94 %, and 82.48 ± 1.11 %, respectively, underscoring the wound healing-promoting effects of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane we developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HE and Masson's trichrome staining analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome staining techniques were utilized to assess the histological status of the wounds during the healing process, as depicted in Fig. 11 . Prior to conducting pathological skin sectioning on rats, we evaluated the in vivo safety of the BC membrane, Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane. The outcomes are illustrated in Fig. S7. Examination of the major organs from each group revealed normal structures without significant pathological changes, with no observable difference between the experimental and control groups. These results indicate satisfactory in vivo biocompatibility of the materials used for wound coverage.

Following this, skin samples were collected on the seventh and fourteenth days and subjected to H&E staining to evaluate the extent of epithelialization in the healed skin. As shown in Fig. 11(a), it was evident that the epithelial layer of the skin in both the BC and control groups remained incompletely healed by the seventh day. Conversely, the Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 groups displayed fully formed epithelial layers at this time point. By day 14, the wound site of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group exhibited the most substantial epidermal healing, with the smallest granulation tissue gap and the presence of a complete stratum corneum.

The wound tissue was stained with Masson's trichrome to determine the amount of collagen present. All groups exhibited collagen deposition on the fourteenth day, as shown in Fig. 11(a). Of these, the wounds in the blank control groups and BC group showed loose collagen deposition. Wounds treated with Cu(II)-RCDs160, and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 showed enhanced collagen fiber organization as well as thicker and denser collagen deposition.

To assess the ROS scavenging capability in vivo, newly formed tissues collected on the third day were stained with the ROS fluorescent probe dihydroethylenediamine (DHE) to quantify the ROS content in the wound bed. Both the BC group and the control group displayed pronounced fluorescence intensity and vivid red fluorescence signals, as illustrated in Fig. S6. In contrast, there was a significant decrease in the intensity of the red fluorescence signal in the Cu(II)-RCDs160 group. There was hardly any red fluorescence signal visible in the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group. Additional quantitative analysis revealed that the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite group's fluorescence intensity of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite group decreased by approximately 8.3 times compared to that of the control group. This suggests that the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane group can efficiently scavenge ROS to reduce oxidative stress.

The inflammatory stage, the second phase of wound healing, is crucial, and prolonged inflammation can impede the healing process (Jiang et al., 2023). Efficient tissue repair greatly benefits from timely management of the inflammatory microenvironment in the early stages. To ameliorate the inflammatory milieu of chronic wounds, extensive research has been conducted, including the efforts undertaken in this study to shift the macrophages' proinflammatory phenotype (M1) to an anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2). To explore the anti-inflammatory effect of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane during wound repair, we conducted immunohistochemical (IHC) staining of iNOS and CD206 on wound slices from day 7 (Fig. 11(b)). Compared to the control group and BC group, the expression of iNOS (M1 marker) in the wound slices from the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane group was significantly lower. Additionally, the infiltration and distribution of CD206 (M2 marker) were enhanced, indicating increased secretion of anti-inflammatory mediators. These findings validate that the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane can induce the transition of macrophages to an anti-inflammatory phenotype, thereby facilitating subsequent angiogenesis and wound regeneration.

Another vital parameter for evaluating wound healing is angiogenesis. Enhanced angiogenesis can provide cells with a greater nutrient supply and expedite their migration to wounds, thereby promoting collagen synthesis, granulation tissue formation, and wound healing. Hence, we conducted CD31 and VEGF in tissues to assess the angiogenic potential of BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 (Wang et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 11(b), the vascular walls of the rats in the Cu(II)-RCDs160 group and the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group exhibited greater CD31 and VEGF expression than did those in the control and BC groups. Additionally, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group displayed more vascular walls than did the control group. This finding suggested that the endothelial cells in the defect site vascular walls of the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 group proliferated and migrated more quickly, resulting in quicker vascular repair.

All the in vivo animal experiments conducted suggest that the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane, as a novel wound dressing, exerts diverse therapeutic effects on infectious wound defects. These effects include antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and angiogenic properties.

In this study, we initially synthesized copper-doped resveratrol carbon dots using a one-step hydrothermal method. Analysis via XPS, FTIR, and SEM confirmed the successful incorporation of Cu(II)) into the CDs. Subsequently, we developed a multifunctional wound dressing, the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs composite membrane, by impregnating the BC membrane with Cu(II)-RCDs160. SEM, FTIR, and XRD results further validated that BC effectively incorporated Cu(II)-RCDs160, featuring carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups in its structure due to hydrogen bonding interactions. Following the surface functionalization of Cu(II)-RCDs160, the tensile strength, hydrophilicity, adhesion property, and thermal stability of the BC composite membrane were significantly enhanced. Additionally, both Cu(II)-RCDs160 and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes exhibited potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. In vitro experiments demonstrated that Cu(II)-RCDs and BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membranes displayed good blood compatibility, exerted, had no significant cytotoxic effects on HUVECs, L929, or RAW264.7 cells, and showed excellent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and angiogenic abilities. Moreover, in vivo, experiments illustrated that the BC/Cu(II)-RCDs160 composite membrane exhibited superior therapeutic effects compared to the BC membrane in a rat skin full-thickness defect model. This was previously attributed primarily to its ability to promote connective tissue remodeling and epithelialization, enhance collagen expression, inhibit inflammation, and foster neovascularization, the effects of which appear to be due to activation of MAPK and VEGF signaling pathways in vivo and in vitro. In short, this work significantly contributes to clinically relevant wound treatment by presenting a collaborative treatment approach that effectively promotes wound restoration.
